segments.s566-g65: به + noun marks the indirect object: 'to the torturer'. فرمود is the past narrative verb 'he said/commanded'. تا introduces a subordinate purpose/command clause ('so that' / 'in order that'), and گردنش is the noun 'his neck' with the enclitic ـش ('his') attached.
segments.s566-g66: زند here is the verbal action (past/subjunctive/imperative sense 'strike' or 'let him strike') and governs the remainder of the clause; به آتش اندر is an older poetic prepositional phrase 'into/into the fire' (به = into; اندر = in/within). بسوزد is a subjunctive/purpose form 'may it burn' (used as the commanded result) and تنش = 'his body' with the enclitic ـش 'his'.
segments.s566-g67: سر طایر is a genitive-like noun phrase 'the head of Tair/the bird' (سر = head, طایر here a noun following it). از ننگ در خون is a compact poetic expression ('from shame, into blood' or 'stained with blood from shame') where از marks cause/reason and در + خون indicates the bloody result. کشید is the simple past 'drew/pulled/tore'.
segments.s566-g68: دو کتف وی is 'his two shoulders' (کتف = shoulder, وی = 'his/that one'); از پشت بیرون indicates direction/location 'out from the back'. کشید is again the past verb 'pulled/out'. The object (دو کتف) appears before/after the verb in poetic word order, but the meaning is 'he pulled both shoulders out of his back.'
segments.s566-g69: هرانکس 'whoever/anyone' begins an interrogative/exclamatory clause; کجا یافتی is a past interrogative 'where did (you) find', with یافتی second-person past of یافتن. از عرب specifies the group 'from the Arabs.' The whole phrase asks rhetorically where one could find any of the Arabs.
segments.s566-g70: نماندی is the past of ماندن 'remained/was left' (here 'there remained/not left'); که introduces a relative/explanatory clause. با کس = 'with anyone/to anyone', and گشادی دو لب literally 'opened two lips' — a Persian idiom for 'spoke' — where گشادی is an adjectival/predicative form of 'open' and لب 'lip(s)'.
segments.s566-g71: ز indicates 'from' (poetic variant of از), دو دست او = 'his two hands', دور کردی is the past verb 'you removed/put away' (کردی = you did), and دو کفت is the direct object 'the two palms' (کفت = palm). Poetic word order places some constituents before the verb.
segments.s566-g72: جهان ماند is a nominative phrase 'the world remained/was left'; از کار او gives the cause/target 'at his deed' (from/of his action), and در شگفت is a locative expression 'in wonder/astonishment'. Together they mean 'the world was left amazed at his action.'
segments.s566-g73: This is an older Persian word order where the given name/epithet comes first: 'عرابی ذوالاکتاف' is the title/epithet being assigned. 'کردش' is کرد (to make/do) with the enclitic object pronoun -ش = 'him' — literally 'they made him'. 'لقب' is the noun 'title/epithet' and here names what was made; its position after the verb is poetic/archaic but normal in older Persian.
segments.s566-g74: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'. 'از' is the preposition 'from/out of'. 'مهره' here denotes a small object on a string (a bead/amulet); 'بگشاد' is the past form meaning 'opened' (from گشودن, here used as a simple past). 'کفت عرب' should be read as 'کفِ عرب' = 'the Arab's palm' (کفت = کف, 'palm'); so the clause describes the moment 'when from the bead the Arab’s palm opened' — i.e. when he opened/revealed something with his hand.
segments.s566-g75: 'وزانجا' = 'and/from there' (compound of و + از + آنجا). 'یگه شد' literally 'became other' but here functions idiomatically as 'again/once more he became/moved' (شد = become/go, with یگه giving the sense of another/again). 'سوی پارس' = 'toward Pars (Persia)'. 'باز' at the end reinforces 'again' — a common poetic repetition for emphasis.
segments.s566-g76: 'جهانی همه' = 'the whole/worldly everyone' (an emphatic phrase meaning 'all the world/everyone'). 'برد' here is a verb meaning 'carried/took/brought' (archaic past of بردن). 'پیشش' = 'before him / in his presence' (پیش + enclitic -ش). 'نماز' = 'prayer'; the phrase means that all the world brought/paid him prayer/respect.
segments.s566-g77: Read 'برین' as the fused form of 'بر این' = 'upon this / regarding this' (بر + ین, where 'ین' is the older/poetic form of 'این'). 'نیز' = 'also / likewise'. 'بگذشت' is the past of گذشتن meaning 'passed' (time passed or something transpired). 'چندی' = 'for a while'; it modifies the verb to mean 'some time passed'. 'سپهر' = 'the sky / heavens', here the poetic agent whose passing marks the lapse of time.
segments.s566-g78: 'وزان' = 'and from that / thence'. 'پس' = 'then / after that'. 'دگرگونه' = 'different / changed'. 'بنمود' is the past of نمودن (نمود = showed, caused to appear) meaning 'he displayed' or 'he showed'. 'چهر' = 'face / countenance' — together 'he then showed a changed face' (i.e. he altered his expression/appearance).
segments.s566-g33: سپه را is the direct object (را marks 'the army'). همه سر به سر is an idiomatic modifier meaning 'one and all' / 'all together' placed before the verb for emphasis. گرد کرد is a compound with کرد (past-tense 'did/made') where گرد (here verbal/adverbial) conveys the sense of bringing together or moving around — together the phrase means 'he mustered/gathered'. Word order reflects classical Persian: object + modifiers + verb.
segments.s566-g34: گزین کرد is a simple past verb phrase 'he chose/selected' (کرد as past auxiliary). مردان ننگ و نبرد is the direct object: 'men of ننگ and نبرد' — a compact noun phrase where ننگ and نبرد qualify the kind of men chosen. The coordinating و links the two nouns.
segments.s566-g35: به باره is a temporal/occasional phrase 'at the time/occasion' or 'for the attack'; برآورد is a past verb 'he brought forth/caused to come up' (here transitive), and چندی سوار means 'several riders/horsemen' (چندی = some/several, سوار = mounted men). The phrase follows Persian order where the temporal phrase precedes the verb and its object.
segments.s566-g36: هرانکس که بود is a relative clause 'whoever was'; که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' and بود is the past of 'to be'. از در کارزار is a locative/agentive phrase 'from/in the field of battle' (از ... در + noun is a compact classical idiom meaning 'in/experienced with the field of combat'). The clause qualifies which riders were brought forth: those who were (men) of the battlefield.
segments.s566-g37: به دژ در شد is a compact verbal phrase: به + دژ ('to/into the fortress') + در (here 'gate/entrance') + شد (past of 'become/go' used to indicate entrance) — together meaning 'he entered the citadel/fortress'. و connects the next clause. کشتن is the masdar/infinitive 'killing' and اندرگرفت (literally 'took in hand') is a past verb meaning 'began/undertook' so کشتن اندرگرفت = 'he began to kill'.
segments.s566-g38: همه گنجهای کهن is the noun phrase 'all the old treasures' (همه = all, گنجهای = treasures, کهن = ancient). برگرفت is a past-tense verb meaning 'took away / removed' (بر-گرفتن). The whole clause says he seized/removed all the old treasures.
segments.s566-g39: سپه بود is a simple past 'the army/host was'. با طایر is a prepositional phrase 'with birds' (با = with, طایر = birds/messengers — here literal 'birds' is most straightforward). اندر حصار is a locative 'within the enclosure/fortress' (اندر = in/within, حصار = enclosure/fortification). Word order places the copula بود after the subject as in classical Persian.
segments.s566-g40: همه مست خفته is a compact verbless/nominal clause meaning 'all intoxicated (مست) and asleep (خفته)'. فزون از هزار is a quantifier phrase 'more than a thousand' (فزون = in excess, از = 'than' in this comparative use, هزار = thousand). Together they give the state and approximate number of those within the enclosure.
segments.s566-g49: ببود is an archaic past copula meaning 'it was'; it sets the scene. "آن شب و بامداد پگاه" is a coordinated temporal phrase ("that night and the dawn morning") following the verb — Persian often allows the verb before a temporal noun phrase here to give a poetic cadence.
segments.s566-g50: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خورشید is the subject and بنمود is a past transitive verb 'displayed/showed' (poetic register). زرین کلاه is the direct object phrase (زرین = adjective after the noun 'کلاه'), i.e. 'a golden cap/helmet' that the sun 'showed'.
segments.s566-g51: یکی is the indefinite introducer 'a certain' (here simply 'a'). تخت پیروزه is a noun + postpositive adjective pattern ('a turquoise throne') and اندر حصار is the locative prepositional phrase 'inside the fortress' following the NP, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s566-g52: به آیین is a prepositional phrase 'with ceremony / according to custom'. نهادند is the past plural of نهادن 'they placed/installed'. The conjunction و links the next clause: دادند بار = 'they gave/placed a load' (the object of دادند is elided — typical poetic ellipsis, so 'they loaded it' or 'they bestowed a burden/goods').
segments.s566-g53: چو is the temporal 'when'. از + بارپردخته is a prepositional phrase where بارپردخته (a compound adjective/participle) describes being 'finished/settled with the baggage' (poetic, participial use). شد is the past copula and شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb (verb–subject order is common in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s566-g54: به نزدیک او is a prepositional phrase 'to/near him' (به + noun). شد is here an intransitive past verb meaning 'came/arrived' or 'became present', and گل نوبهار ('the flower of the new spring') is the noun phrase that functions as the entity that approached — Persian often places the verb before the noun phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s566-g55: ز here functions as 'of/from' to indicate material or composition ('of ruby'). یاقوت سرخ افسری is a noun phrase 'a ruby‑red diadem' (سرخ follows یاقوت as its descriptive adjective); بر سرش is the locative 'on his head' (بر + سر + possessive clitic ش).
segments.s566-g56: درفشان is an adjective/participle 'shining, glittering'. ز + زربفت چینی describes the material 'of Chinese gold-brocade'. The final token برش in the given segmentation does not match the listed senses (it looks like a fused form meaning 'upon him' or a copyist compression); here it functions to place the shining brocade on/over him (I mark its sense as not listed).
segments.s566-g9: Structure: a direct object with the definite marker RA (باده‌پرستنده را) followed by a verb phrase. پرستنده باده (literally "worshipper/devotee of wine") is the noun phrase being summoned; را marks it as the definite direct object. پیش خواند is a compound verbal idea: پیش ('before / to the front / in the presence of') + خواند (past of خواندن here used in the sense 'call, summon'). The overall meaning is 'he called/summoned the wine‑devotee forward.'
segments.s566-g10: Adverbial phrase + object + verb: به خوبی functions adverbially ('well / eloquently'); سخنها is the plural object 'words / speeches'; فراوان is an adverb/adjunct modifying the verb ('abundantly / in large amount'); براند is the main verb (classical present/3rd‑sg form) meaning 'bring forth / utter / put forth' (from راندن used figuratively). Together: 'he uttered many words well and abundantly.'
segments.s566-g11: بدو is an indirect object pronoun meaning 'to him/unto him'; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' introducing reported speech; کامشب ('this night / tonight') is a time adverb; تویی is the copular 2nd‑person ('you are'); باده‌ده is a compound noun 'wine‑giver' (cupbearer) used here as the role assigned to the addressee. Word order places the indirect object before گفت (classical style) and then gives the reported clause.
segments.s566-g12: Imperative/optative sentence: به marks the beneficiary/recipient ('to/for'); طایر here 'bird' (often figurative 'messenger' but literal 'bird' fits the scene); همه 'all' distributes the gift; باده ساده ('simple/plain wine') is the object to be given; ده is the 2nd‑person verb of دادن ('give') used as an instruction/command. Together: 'Give plain wine to all the birds.'
segments.s566-g13: Purpose clause introduced by تا ('so that' / 'in order that'). همان at the start points back to the previously mentioned wine/arrangement ('that same' / 'so'). بدارند is a subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person plural of داشتن ('have/hold') meaning 'may have/hold'; باده به دست = 'wine in (their) hand(s)' (به + دست = 'in the hand' / 'at hand'). The clause expresses purpose: 'so that they may hold wine in their hands.'
segments.s566-g14: بدان is a compact/archaic connective (ب‑ + دان) referring back to 'that' or 'with that' (see note); تا again marks purpose. بخسپند is an archaic present/subjunctive plural meaning 'may fall asleep' (from classical خسبیدن used where Modern Persian would use خوابیدن). و = 'and'. گردند is subjunctive/optative 3rd‑pl of گشتن 'become', and مست is the predicate 'drunk/intoxicated'. Overall: 'with that (wine) so that they fall asleep and become drunk.'
segments.s566-g15: Word order: بدو ('to him') precedes گفت ('said'), and ساقی is the subject 'the cupbearer'. که introduces reported content. من بنده‌ام is the reported clause: من 'I' + بنده (servant) + ـام (1st‑person copula enclitic) = 'I am a servant' (a humble self‑address). The whole segment reports the cupbearer's statement of devotion.
segments.s566-g16: Prepositional phrase + locative + predicate: به فرمان تو = 'by/through your command' (به as agentive/means 'by'); در جهان = 'in the world' (here 'in this life'); زنده‑ام = 'I am alive / I live' (زنده + enclitic ـام). The clause states that his living/existence is by the addressee's command.
segments.s566-g1: ز خاور چو خورشید بنمود تاج is a temporal clause: ز (a poetic preposition) = “from/coming from,” خاور = “the east,” چو = temporal conjunction “when,” خورشید = “the sun,” and بنمود is a past tense verb (3rd person) meaning “showed / displayed.” تاج is the object “crown.” Word order follows classical Persian: prepositional phrase + temporal conjunction + subject + verb + object.
segments.s566-g2: گل زرد شد بر زمین رنگ ساج: گل = “flower(s),” زرد شد is a verb phrase (زرد + شد) meaning “became yellow” (شد = past of شدن, here lexical ‘become’). بر زمین = “upon the ground/earth” (بر + زمین), and رنگ ساج = “the color/hue of the saj (tree)” where رنگ is the noun “color” modified by ساج. Note the poetic omission of linking particles; the clause states result/effect following the sunrise clause.
segments.s566-g3: ز گنجور دستور بستد کلید: ز = “from/at” (poetic), گنجور = “treasury/treasury-house,” دستور = “an order/command,” and بستد is a past-tense verb (3rd sing.) meaning “he shut/fastened/caused to be locked.” کلید is the direct object “key.” The verb بستد pairs with دستور to mean “gave the order to fasten/lock.”
segments.s566-g4: خورش خانه و خمهای نبید: خورش‌خانه (compound) = “kitchen” (خورش = cooked dishes; خانه = house/place), و = “and,” خم‌های = plural “jars/earthenware vessels” (خم with plural ending), and نبید = “wine.” In poetry the ezāfe linking is sometimes omitted; here خمهای نبید = “jars of wine.” These nouns complete/specify کلید (the key of the kitchen) and the things to be fastened.
segments.s566-g5: بدژدر هرانکس که بد مهتری: بدژدر is treated as a place or name (proper noun) introducing the scene. هرانکس که = relative phrase “whoever/anyone who,” بد here functions adverbially “badly/poorly,” and مهتری is “preeminence/chiefship.” Grammatically this is an elliptical relative clause: it sets up “whoever, by poor preeminence…,” with the predicate realized or implied in the next segment.
segments.s566-g6: وزان جنگیان رنج‌دیده سری: وزان = “and/from that” linking to what precedes; جنگیان here denotes participants in war (poetic plural form implying “warriors” or “fighters”), رنج‌دیده = adjectival perfect participle “suffering/war‑worn,” and سری = “a head” or “chief.” The phrase reads as a nominal continuation of the relative clause: “(became) the chief among those war‑worn warriors.” The verb is omitted (elliptical) as is common in classical verse.
segments.s566-g7: خورشها فرستاد و چندی نبید: خورشها is the plural object “dishes/stews,” فرستاد is a past-tense verb (3rd sing.) “he sent/dispatched,” و = “and,” چندی = “some/several,” and نبید = “wine.” The verb فرستاد governs both objects (the dishes and the wine); the subject is implied (“he”).
segments.s566-g8: هم از بویها نرگس و شنبلید: هم = “also/even,” از = “from/of,” بویها = “scents/odours,” and the two nouns نرگس و شنبلید name the scents (narcissus and shenbulid). Again the verb is omitted and understood from the previous clause (i.e., he also sent scents of narcissus and shenbulid).
segments.s566-g17: 'چو' is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'; 'خورشید' is the grammatical subject 'the sun'; 'بر باختر گشت' is a verb phrase 'turned toward the west' (preposition 'بر' + direction 'باختر' + past verbal gash(t)); 'زرد' is a predicative adjective describing the sun's colour and appears after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s566-g18: 'شب تیره' is the noun phrase 'the dark night' (noun + adjective). 'گفتش' is the past-tense verb 'said' with enclitic object 'ـش' = 'to him/it' (so 'the night said to him'). 'که' introduces the reported clause ('that ...'). 'از راه برد' is the subordinate clause meaning '(he) went/was led off the way' — here 'از' is the ablative 'from', 'راه' = 'way/path'. The verb 'برد' in the printed senses list does not include the relevant past-tense sense of بردن ('led/took/caused to go'), so its sense index is given as -1 below.
segments.s566-g19: 'می‌خسروی' is an epithet/title referring to the 'wine‑king' or a named person (here treated as the subject). 'خواست' is the simple past 'desired/wanted'. 'طایر' is the direct object 'the bird'. 'به جام' is a prepositional phrase 'for/to the cup' indicating purpose or destination (literally 'into/for the cup').
segments.s566-g20: 'نخستین' here modifies the following clause as 'first/foremost'. 'ز' is the poetic form of 'from'. 'غسانیان' names the people/group 'the Ghassanids' (genitive/ablative complement after 'ز'). 'برد' functions as a past verb 'took/won' (classical poetic usage of بردن), but that verbal sense is not present in the supplied sense list so its index is -1. 'نام' is used as 'renown/reputation' (not only a literal name).
segments.s566-g21: 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. 'بگذشت' is past tense 'passed' (here of time). 'یک پاس' is 'one pass/one watch' (a time unit) — this meaning is not one of the supplied senses for 'پاس', so its sense index is -1. 'از تیره شب' is an ablative/genitive phrase 'of the dark night' specifying which watch passed.
segments.s566-g22: 'بیاسود' is a predicative adjective/verb form meaning 'not at rest / uneasy' (it describes the bird's state). 'طایر' is the noun 'the bird' (subject of this predicate). 'ز بانگ جلب' is an ablative phrase 'from the cry/sound of attraction' giving the cause — 'ز' = 'from', 'بانگ' = 'cry/sound', 'جلب' = 'attraction/lure'. Word order places the predicate before the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s566-g23: 'برفتند' is the third-person plural past 'they went/departed'. 'یکسر' is an adverb meaning 'all together/entirely' modifying the verb. 'سوی خوابگاه' is a directional phrase 'toward the sleeping quarters' (سوی = toward, خوابگاه = sleeping place/quarters).
segments.s566-g24: This is a poetic object–verb–subject order: 'پرستندگان را' marks 'the worshippers' as the definite object ('را'), 'بفرمود' is the honorific past 'he commanded/said' (here 'bade/ordered'), and 'شاه' is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for emphasis. So the clause means 'the king ordered the worshippers.'
segments.s566-g25: This is a relative clause introduced by که (that/who) — که begins the clause. با کس نگوید is an idiomatic negative: with + کس (anyone) + نگوید (does not speak) — here نگوید is a negative present/past-style verb form used poetically for 'does not say/speak'. سخن (speech) follows as the object; جز براز (except dung/filth) marks the sole content of that speech. Word order is somewhat poetic (verb before its object in places), and جز signals exception.
segments.s566-g26: نهانی is an adverb ('secretly') modifying the verb phrase that follows. در دژ is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/at the fortress'); گشادند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb used poetically for 'opened' (an older/poetic form rather than modern گشودند). باز here means 'again', so together the phrase says they secretly opened the fortress gates again.
segments.s566-g27: بدان here is the fused prepositional form (به + آن) meaning 'with regard to that' or 'toward that'; it introduces the reference. شاه شاپور is the subject ('King Shapur'), خود is emphatic 'himself', and چشم‌داشت is a noun meaning 'expectation/claim' that functions as what he 'had' — the possession is completed in the next segment by the verb داشت.
segments.s566-g28: از introduces the cause or source ('because of' / 'on account of') followed by آواز مستان ('the singing/voice of the revelers/drunkards') as the provocation. به دل خشم داشت is an idiomatic Persian sequence: به دل = 'in his heart' and خشم داشت = 'he had anger' (داشت is the past-tense possession verb 'had'). The whole segment gives the reason and the resulting feeling.
segments.s566-g29: چو is a temporal/simile conjunction meaning 'when' or 'as'; شمع is the simile 'like a candle'. از در دژ is the source/location 'from the fortress gate'. بیفروخت is a poetic past verb meaning to blaze/flare forth or be lit (an archaic/poetic form), and گفت is the speech verb 'he said', which introduces the reported clause beginning with که in the next segment.
segments.s566-g30: که introduces reported speech ('that'): گشتیم is first-person-plural past 'we became' or 'we turned out to be'. با marks accompaniment/instrumentality ('by/with'), here با بخت بیدار = 'by/with an awakened/wakeful fortune' (بخت 'fate/fortune' + بیدار 'awake' as an adjectival phrase). جفت is the predicate noun 'pair'; together the phrase means 'we have become, by fortune, an awakened pair.' Word order places the instrumental phrase before the predicate.
segments.s566-g31: مر is an archaic clitic/pronoun form (here glossed as 'me'/'to me' in the supplied senses) appearing before the noun phrase; آن ماه‌رخ را is the definite object 'that moon-faced (one)' with the object marker را. به + پرده‌سرای is a dative/locative phrase 'to the curtain-chamber' (purdah-room / women's apartment in context). The whole segment names who was sent and where.
segments.s566-g32: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (polite past). تا here means 'so that/in order that' and introduces a purpose clause. خوب کرد(ند) جای = 'they should make (or made) a good place': خوب modifies جای ('a good/beautiful place'), and کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' agreeing with an implied agent (the household/attendants).
segments.s566-g41: دگر here = 'the other / the remaining' (not 'again'); it picks out the rest of those previously mentioned. خفته آسیمه is a noun (خفته, 'asleep') followed by an adverbial/adjectival modifier (آسیمه, 'in a panic / hasty'), and برخاستند is a simple past (3rd person plural) 'they rose / sprang up' placed at the end as is normal in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s566-g42: به هر جای جنگی is a prepositional phrase 'to/into every place of battle' (هر + جای with the postpositive adjective جنگی 'warlike / of battle'), and بیاراستند is a 3rd person plural past 'they arrayed / equipped (themselves)' with the verb at the clause end; together the clause means they prepared/armed themselves throughout the battlefield.
segments.s566-g43: ازیشان کس functions as 'none of them' because the verb is negated: کس (here understood as 'no one') + ننمود (negative past). از بیم is an ablative/causal phrase 'out of/because of fear' (از + بیم). ننمود is the past of نمود 'to show' with the negative prefix ن- so 'did not show', and پشت ('back') completes the idiom پشت نمودن 'to turn one's back / flee'.
segments.s566-g44: بسی here is a classical adverb meaning 'much / many / very'; نامور شاه ایران is the noun phrase 'the renowned king of Iran' (نامور post‑nominal adjective). بکشت is an archaic/past form meaning 'he slew / killed' (classical Persian often uses a prefixed b‑ in past forms); the clause is a simple past (3rd singular).
segments.s566-g45: چو شد marks a temporal clause 'when' + past 'became/was'. طایر is the subject 'the bird', اندر کف او is a locative phrase 'in his palm', and اسیر is a predicative adjective/participial adjective 'captive' (i.e. 'the bird became captive in his palm'). The copulative/auxiliary شد links the predicate to the subject.
segments.s566-g46: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. The following words are adjectival/adverbial modifiers placed after the verb in classical style: برهنه 'naked', دوان (present participle/adverb) 'running/rushed', and ناگزیر 'compelled / inevitably (forced)'. Together they describe how that one arrived: 'came running, naked and forced.'
segments.s566-g47: به چنگ وی آمد is an idiomatic phrase 'came into his grasp' (به + چنگ + pronoun + آمد = fell into his hand / he seized). حصار و بنه are the nouns 'fort/enclosure' and (poetically) 'camp / heap / encampment' respectively; the coordination with و links them as the things captured. The verb آمد is used causatively/figuratively here to mean 'was taken.'
segments.s566-g48: گرفتار شد is a compound meaning 'became captured / were taken' (شد as auxiliary with گرفتار). مردم بدتنه is the noun + postpositive epithet 'the ill‑favored / ill‑bodied people' (adjective after the noun, as is typical); note the subject phrase follows the verbal predicate, a frequent ordering in Ferdowsi's verse.
segments.s566-g57: بدانست is a past-tense, third-person singular verb meaning 'he realized/knew.' کای is a vocative particle ('O, ...') addressing the king and is inserted here for emphasis; it does not change the verb's subject. جادوی is the construct form of جادو + ـی and functions as a genitive/attributive ('the magic/sorcery of...'); کار اوست is a nominal predicate ('is his work'), with او as the possessor and ست (است) as the copula.
segments.s566-g58: بدو is an indirect-object prepositional clitic phrase meaning 'to him/for him' placed before the verb for emphasis. بد رسیدن uses بد (bad/evil) with رسیدن ('to reach/arrive') to mean 'evil would befall' or 'ill would come.' ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'because of / from,' and کردار اوست is a nominal clause 'it is from his conduct' — altogether 'evil will come to him from his deeds.'
segments.s566-g59: چنین گفت is a conventional opening 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); the verb is perfect/past. کای شاه آزاد مرد is a vocative phrase addressing the king: کای ('O'), شاه ('king'), آزاد مرد ('noble man' — an epithet; آزاد modifies مرد). The vocative cluster is set off from the reporting verb.
segments.s566-g60: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه ('a watch/look') + کن (imperative 'do') = 'look!' or 'keep watch.' The repeated که که is an older/poetic double conjunction introducing the subordinate clause ('that/for/see that'); the second که often just leads the clause. فرزند با من چه کرد is the reported clause 'what the son did to me' — با with here indicates relation toward/against the speaker, چه کرد is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what did (he) do.'
segments.s566-g61: چنین گفت introduces reported speech ('thus said'). شاپور is the speaker (proper name). بدنام را uses بدنام ('notorious/disgraced') as the (addressed) object/topic; را is the object/topic marker marking the person being addressed or referred to. The word order is verb + addressee marked by را, typical in classical narrative.
segments.s566-g62: که introduces the content of what was said ('that/which/who'). از پرده is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the curtain' (از = 'from'), پرده = 'curtain/veil.' چون is a simile 'like/as,' and دخت بهرام را is the comparison object 'Bahram's maiden' (دخت = maiden/daughter, بهرام = name); را again is the object/topic marker. The fragment is a simile fragment leading into the next clause.
segments.s566-g63: بیاری here denotes '(a) companion / ally' (literally 'with-help' as a noun) and is paired with و ('and') to link two results. رسوا کنی is a verb phrase in subjunctive/imperative form 'you disgrace' or 'you will make ... disgraced' (کنی = second-person subjunctive/optative of کردن). دوده را is the direct object marked by را; دوده is literally 'soot/smoke' but may be used metaphorically or as a name here — grammatically it is the object of رسوا کنی.
segments.s566-g64: برانگیزی is second-person singular (subjunctive/future-like) 'you will arouse/you arouse.' آن is the demonstrative 'that/the.' کین آسوده is a nominal phrase 'the enmity/hatred that is at rest/settled' (کین = hatred/vengeance; آسوده = 'at peace/settled'); را marks it as the object. Together: 'you will rouse that settled hatred.'

sentences.s566-s33: He ordered the torturer to strike off his neck and to burn his body in the fire.
sentences.s566-s34: He tore off Tair's head so that it streamed blood from shame; he pulled his two shoulders out from his back.
sentences.s566-s35: Where did you find anyone among the Arabs? There was none left who had opened his lips to another.
sentences.s566-s36: You removed both palms from his two hands; the world was left astonished at his deed.
sentences.s566-s37: They gave him the epithet “Arabi, Zul‑aktāf” (the broad‑shouldered Arab) when the Arab opened his palm from the bead.
sentences.s566-s38: From there he once more turned toward Pars; the whole world brought prayers before him.
sentences.s566-s39: For a while the heavens likewise passed over this; after that he put on a changed countenance.
sentences.s566-s17: He mustered the whole army together and selected the men for honor and battle.
sentences.s566-s18: He brought forth, at each occasion, several horsemen — whoever was experienced in the field of battle.
sentences.s566-s19: He entered the fortress and began to slay; he removed all the old treasures.
sentences.s566-s20: The host was inside the enclosure with birds; all were drunk and asleep, numbering more than a thousand.
sentences.s566-s25: It was that night and the early dawn when the sun displayed a golden cap.
sentences.s566-s26: They placed a turquoise throne inside the fortress with ceremony and set/put a load (on it).
sentences.s566-s27: When the king was done with the baggage, the fresh-spring flower came near him.
sentences.s566-s28: A ruby-red diadem rested on his head; a glittering piece (standard) of Chinese gold-brocade shone over him.
sentences.s566-s5: He summoned the wine‑worshipper forward, and he uttered many fine words eloquently.
sentences.s566-s6: He said to him, 'Tonight you are the wine‑giver; give plain wine to all the birds.'
sentences.s566-s7: So that they may hold the wine in their hands, so that they may fall asleep and become drunk.
sentences.s566-s8: To him the cupbearer said, 'I am your servant; by your command I live in the world.'
sentences.s566-s1: From the east, when the sun displayed its crown, the flowers turned yellow and the earth took on the hue of the saj tree.
sentences.s566-s2: From the treasury he gave the order to fasten the key of the kitchen and the wine‑jars.
sentences.s566-s3: In Badždar, whoever, through poor preeminence, (became) the chief among those war‑worn fighters.
sentences.s566-s4: He sent dishes and some wine, and also (sent) scents — narcissus and shenbulid (a fragrant plant).
sentences.s566-s9: When the sun turned toward the west and grew yellow, the dark night said to him that he had gone off the road.
sentences.s566-s10: The wine‑king desired the bird for the cup; first he won renown among the Ghassanids.
sentences.s566-s11: When one watch of the dark night had passed, the bird, uneasy from the cry of the lure, was restless.
sentences.s566-s12: They all went together toward the sleeping quarters; the king bade the worshippers.
sentences.s566-s13: Who spoke to no one, saying nothing but filth; secretly they opened the fortress gate again.
sentences.s566-s14: That King Shapur himself had expectations (an aim regarding that); he harbored anger in his heart because of the revelers' singing.
sentences.s566-s15: When, like a candle, he flared forth from the fortress gate, he said that 'we have become, by fortune, an awakened pair.'
sentences.s566-s16: He ordered that moon-faced one to the curtain-chamber (purdah-room), so that they might make a good place.
sentences.s566-s21: The remaining sleepers, distraught, sprang up and arrayed themselves for battle everywhere.
sentences.s566-s22: None of them, out of fear, turned their back; the famed king of Iran slew many.
sentences.s566-s23: When the bird was captured in his palm, one came running, naked and compelled.
sentences.s566-s24: The fort and the camp came into his grasp, and the ill‑favored people were taken captive.
sentences.s566-s29: He realized, O king, that this was the work of magic, and that evil would befall him because of his conduct.
sentences.s566-s30: Thus he said, 'O king, noble man, look and see what the son has done to me.'
sentences.s566-s31: Thus Shapur spoke to the notorious man, saying: 'who, from behind the curtain, like Bahram's maiden...'
sentences.s566-s32: You will win a companion and disgrace Dodeh; you will rouse that settled hatred.
